content.full_text:
September 19, 1991

Greg Gonot, Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Informational Train Tour

Dear Greg:

We are seeking written confirmation of telephone advice on the application to a trip on a waste disposal train of the exception from the definition of gift for informational materials. As we discussed, the train travels between a central solid waste collection point in Los Angeles to a disposal site near Amboy in the desert. To provide information about the concept of disposal of solid waste via train, tours from the collection point to the desert disposal site are offered to environmentalists, public officials, community activists and others who are considering utilizing this method of disposal or who wish to learn about its effect on their community. There is no commercial train transportation available on this route, and no other mode of transportation can be taken from the collection point to the disposal site and back over the precise route taken by the train.

Under the above circumstances, you advised that transportation on the train fell within the intangible informational materials exception to the definition of gift. (Gov. Code § 82028(b)(1); In re Spellman (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 16; Leidigh Advice No. A-89-248; Rafuse Advice, No. I-91-232.) However, the value of any incidental food and beverage available on the train is a gift and is therefore subject to any applicable limits and reporting requirements. (Rafuse Advice, supra.)

We would appreciate confirmation of this advice at your earliest convenience.

Sincerely,

Charles H. Bell, Jr.

CHB/kah
5697.03
